name: AXA
title: [1mm Finance Talk] Why Shinhan Bank Is Smiling on a Bullish Market... Grabbing Both Fund ...
description:
As a result, fund sales fees have nearly doubled year-on-year, driving growth in non-interest income.
This performance was not achieved simply by riding favorable market conditions; it was made possible because the bank earned strong trust by strictly complying with regulations throughout the fund sales process.
Even as the banking industry faces a shared crisis of deposit outflows, Shinhan Bank appears to be finding a new path through fund sales grounded in trust.
According to the financial sector on February 8, Shinhan Bank’s fund sales balance as of the end of January stood at 23.2738 trillion won.
The bank is also found to be complying with fund sales procedures and regulations better than other financial institutions.
text:
Amid the boom in the domestic stock market, the balance of funds sold by Shinhan Bank has increased by around 6 trillion won over the past year. As a result, fund sales fees have nearly doubled year-on-year, driving growth in non-interest income. This performance was not achieved simply by riding favorable market conditions; it was made possible because the bank earned strong trust by strictly complying with regulations throughout the fund sales process. Even as the banking industry faces a shared crisis of deposit outflows, Shinhan Bank appears to be finding a new path through fund sales grounded in trust.

According to the financial sector on February 8, Shinhan Bank’s fund sales balance as of the end of January stood at 23.2738 trillion won. The key point is its steep growth. The fund balance, which was at 17.1124 trillion won at the end of 2022, increased to 22.9769 trillion won last year, rising by 5.8645 trillion won (34.2%) in just one year. Based on data from the Korea Financial Investment Association, the balance increased by about 4.2 trillion won, the largest gain both in absolute increase and in balance among the five major commercial banks. As a result, fund profit and loss (sales fee income) also surged 48.9%, from 57.6 billion won to 85.8 billion won, contributing to non-interest income growth.

In detail, sales of strategic funds launched last year expanded significantly on the back of high returns. As part of its “Once Again Korea” campaign last year, Shinhan Bank recommended three domestic equity-type strategic funds. The recommended funds were Kyobo AXA Power Index, KCGI Korea, and Baring High Dividend. Their combined sales from early June last year to the present (as of the 3rd) over roughly eight months reached 959 billion won. Over the same period, their returns came to 93.6%, 65.6%, and 53.2%, respectively. Shinhan Bank has now expanded its roster of recommended funds to eight.

The bank is also found to be complying with fund sales procedures and regulations better than other financial institutions. In the annual assessment published by the Korea Financial Consumer Protection Foundation, Shinhan Bank received the highest score for two consecutive years. In last year’s assessment, released early this year, it was the only one among a total of 21 fund distributors, including banks and securities firms, to score over 90 points in the overall evaluation. This contrasts with competitors Hana Bank, KB Kookmin Bank, and Woori Bank, which ranked 12th, 17th, and 18th, respectively, and were rated as “insufficient” or “poor.” The trust earned from customers during the fund sales process has translated directly into results.

This appears to be largely due to Shinhan Bank’s continued reinforcement of a “complete sales” framework from the very first stage of fund distribution. A Shinhan Bank representative said, “To enhance financial consumers’ freedom of choice and level of understanding, we have focused on building a preventive protection framework rather than merely conducting post-sale checks,” adding, “Standardizing sales procedures through an AI system and continuously conducting role-playing training for all employees have had a positive impact.” The representative went on to say, “We see 2026 as the first year of fully embedding complete sales practices,” and added, “We will further advance sales processes across all investment products and move to improve our digital channels.”

name: Nvidia
title: Is AMD's 17% Decline a Warning for Nvidia Shareholders Ahead of Feb. 25?
description:
And investors can count on both of these companies for a clear picture of the current and, potentially, future AI market.
In spite of these results, though, AMD stock sank 17% in the trading session following the report.
Is this stock performance, following a strong earnings report, a warning for Nvidia shareholders ahead of the market giant's Feb. 25 earnings report?
The company is set to report fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter and full-year earnings on Feb. 25 after the stock market closes.
So, AMD's drop may be a warning for Nvidia shareholders in the short term, suggesting the stock may face turbulence even if earnings numbers are strong.
text:
Many players are participating in the high-growth field of artificial intelligence (AI). But two in particular stand out as they make key tools critical to the development and functioning of AI. I'm talking about chip designers Nvidia (NVDA +8.01%) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD +8.32%).

Nvidia is the chip leader, offering the world's most powerful graphics processing units (GPUs), and this has resulted in incredible growth over the past few years. AMD has proven it can compete with this market leader as it's launched chips rivaling those of Nvidia and has been announcing soaring demand and revenue. And investors can count on both of these companies for a clear picture of the current and, potentially, future AI market.

AMD was the first to report quarterly earnings during the current earnings season, and the company announced record revenue for the quarter and the full year, as well as strong profitability. Chief Lisa Su even said that, based on the current situation, AMD expects "significant" sales and revenue growth this year.

In spite of these results, though, AMD stock sank 17% in the trading session following the report. Is this stock performance, following a strong earnings report, a warning for Nvidia shareholders ahead of the market giant's Feb. 25 earnings report? Let's find out.

AMD's soaring earnings

So, first, let's consider AMD's report. As mentioned, overall, the company delivered very positive news. In the quarter, revenue climbed 34% to more than $10 billion, surpassing analysts' estimates. Gross margin expanded to 54% from 51% a year ago, showing greater profitability on sales. And Su said the company is on track to reach its targets, such as increasing revenue at a more than 35% compound annual growth rate over the coming three to five years.

"We are entering a multiyear demand super cycle for high-performance and AI computing," Su said during the earnings call.

Still, the stock tumbled in the following trading session amid disappointment about the company's first-quarter forecast. Though AMD's prediction for about $9.8 billion in revenue beat expectations of $9.3 billion, some analysts were expecting even more growth, especially considering the great demand for AI chips at the moment.

Now, let's turn to Nvidia. The company is set to report fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter and full-year earnings on Feb. 25 after the stock market closes. The chip giant has a strong track record of surpassing analysts' estimates, and in recent times, Nvidia has spoken of soaring demand for its GPUs. The average analyst estimate calls for revenue to climb 67% year over year to more than $65 billion in the quarter.

TSMC speaks of strong demand

Messages from others in the industry also offer us reason to be optimistic. For example, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, the manufacturer of Nvidia's chips, spoke of strong demand when it reported earnings a few weeks ago.

So, it's very likely that Nvidia will deliver a good deal of positive news. But will investors, as in the case of AMD, choose to focus on any potential weakness or risk -- even a general concern about AI spending down the road -- and sanction the stock? It's possible. In recent months, investors have worried about the high valuations of AI stocks and the sustainability of these levels. Concerns about a possible AI bubble even surfaced back in November.

All of this means that, even if Nvidia presents a fantastic report, in the short term, there's a possibility its stock performance may not reflect the positive picture. Now here's the good news: Any short-term turbulence doesn't change the long-term AI landscape or Nvidia's prospects in this market over time. And any dip in the stock is actually positive for us as investors, as it offers us the opportunity to get in on the shares at a reasonable price.

So, AMD's drop may be a warning for Nvidia shareholders in the short term, suggesting the stock may face turbulence even if earnings numbers are strong. But that's OK -- Nvidia still is likely to score a win over the long term, and that's great news for investors who hold onto this AI leader.

name: Barclays
title: High tariffs drive investors away from U.S. hedge funds
description:
Washington, 8 Feb (SANA) U.S. hedge funds are experiencing a decline in investor interest for the first time since 2023, amid the continued impact of high tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump in April, according to a CNN analysis.
Barclays noted that interest in hedge funds in the Asia-Pacific region more than doubled compared to 2024, while European hedge funds also saw a significant rise in investor demand.
European partners of the US have repeatedly warned that high tariffs pose risks to both the global and American economies.
Meanwhile, tariff revenues have fallen short of US Treasury expectations, with little evidence of a revival in domestic manufacturing.
Within the US, consumers have borne the cost through rising prices of basic goods and auto parts.
text:
Washington, 8 Feb (SANA) U.S. hedge funds are experiencing a decline in investor interest for the first time since 2023, amid the continued impact of high tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump in April, according to a CNN analysis.

The report said investors are increasingly shifting away from the US market, which has lost appeal due to trade uncertainty, in favour of Asian and European markets seen as more stable.

A survey by Barclays Bank in London, covering 342 investors managing $7.8 trillion in assets, showed that investors in the US and Europe plan to increase exposure to US hedge funds this year by nearly five percentage points less than in 2025, redirecting part of their investments to Asia and Europe.

Barclays noted that interest in hedge funds in the Asia-Pacific region more than doubled compared to 2024, while European hedge funds also saw a significant rise in investor demand.

European partners of the US have repeatedly warned that high tariffs pose risks to both the global and American economies. Meanwhile, tariff revenues have fallen short of US Treasury expectations, with little evidence of a revival in domestic manufacturing. Within the US, consumers have borne the cost through rising prices of basic goods and auto parts.

title: Middle East credit outlook for 2026 stable despite lower oil: S&P - TradeArabia
description:
The 2026 sovereign credit outlook for the Middle East region is stable thanks to the continued economic and credit resilience despite elevated geopolitical tensions and lower oil prices, according to a report by S&P Global Ratings.
"However, these diversification efforts carry significant fiscal costs, which are amplified by lower oil prices.
Government fiscal response mechanisms to lower oil prices have contributed to stronger ratings over the past two years.
The average rating has risen to 'BBB' from 'BBB-', reflecting policy steps to enhance fiscal flexibility in the event of lower oil prices (and including the 'AA' rated UAE added in mid-year 2025).
For most, fiscal breakeven oil prices have declined materially over this period.
text:
The 2026 sovereign credit outlook for the Middle East region is stable thanks to the continued economic and credit resilience despite elevated geopolitical tensions and lower oil prices, according to a report by S&P Global Ratings.

Strong government balance sheets and increasing policy and fiscal flexibility across much of the region will act as protective buffers to potential shorter and longer-term external pressures.

S&P forecast average regional growth to accelerate slightly to about 3.5%, boosted by hydrocarbon production and strong nonoil activity, particularly in the UAE and Saudi Arabia. Notably, Qatar’s new liquefied natural gas (LNG) production is due to come on stream this year.

Heightened geopolitical risk is unlikely to disrupt credit channels except in the case of severe events, but could deter investment, reinforce fiscal dependence on oil, and maintain expenditure pressures, it stated in the report.

"Our forecasts are underpinned by the assumption that public sector initiatives focused on maximising oil and gas revenues - including infrastructure investments to increase capacity - will maintain growth in tandem with efforts to develop sustainable nonoil private sectors, most visibly in Saudi Arabia," said S&P in the report.

"However, these diversification efforts carry significant fiscal costs, which are amplified by lower oil prices. We assume a $60/bbl oil price in 2026. Our sovereign ratings in the region incorporate the potential for short-term increases in geopolitical stress and some oil price volatility," it stated.

"Key supportive factors remain, including substantial liquid government asset buffers and the region’s history of supporting financially weaker governments. We expect these factors to continue bolstering regional resilience," it added.

Government fiscal response mechanisms to lower oil prices have contributed to stronger ratings over the past two years. The average rating has risen to 'BBB' from 'BBB-', reflecting policy steps to enhance fiscal flexibility in the event of lower oil prices (and including the 'AA' rated UAE added in mid-year 2025).

While the sophistication of government financial planning and policy responses varies across nations, broadening revenue streams remains a common goal, said the top ratings agency in its report.

"We've observed that governments generally have greater financial flexibility than they did during the sharp oil price correction that began in 2014. This is because they've introduced new revenue streams including VAT and corporate tax and have become more transparent about budgeted expenditures that could be reduced if required. For most, fiscal breakeven oil prices have declined materially over this period. However, countries with less robust financial reserves and a greater reliance on commodity revenues, such as Bahrain and, to a lesser extent following

title: Software stocks have been crushed. Here's how to play the sector as the dust settles.
description:

text:
By Hannah Pedone

Analysts see opportunities in shares of cybersecurity, infrastructure and HR software companies

Investors can take a breather and assess whether there are opportunities to buy some beaten-down software stocks, after what some analysts believe was "indiscriminate" selling.

Software stocks have taken a beating, and now investors get a pause to assess the carnage and see whether some parts of the sector have been unfairly punished.

The Shares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF IGV snapped an eight-session losing streak on Friday, but it has still dropped 16.5% since the streak started. The declines over that time were widespread - affecting not just makers of products like specialty software for the legal field, which may be at risk of more imminent disruption from new artificial-intelligence tools, but also shares of cybersecurity and infrastructure plays that analysts think might be more insulated.

The pressure on software stocks intensified this week after Anthropic announced new legal and financial AI features that make performing financial analysis and legal tasks easier for its Claude chatbot. Additionally, OpenAI launched OpenAI Frontier, an enterprise platform to help companies use AI agents to perform actual tasks. These tools added to existing investor fears that AI will upend the business models of traditional software companies.

Yet some analysts say the selling was indiscriminate, based more on fear of the unknown than an actual and serious threat.

"I do believe this is kind of like a DeepSeek moment," Needham analyst Scott Berg told MarketWatch - referring to the time in early 2025 when the tech sector tumbled amid worries about cheap AI competition from China, which never really played out. "It's mainly because if we look at the software functionality that I think investors are worried about, most of that just hasn't come to fruition."

Whether the threat is real or not, Wall Street has decided that the once-lofty valuations within the software sector were in need of a reset, especially if it becomes harder for the companies to take on debt because of their reliance on aging subscription business models for steady streams of revenue.

Friday's stock-market bounce and the weekend break have provided investors the chance to assess whether the bearish narrative could last. Some, like John Belton, portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, believes the worries about AI agents replacing software, especially for industry-specific functions, are overblown.

Belton wrote in a note that for software, it was never the code itself which was the "secret sauce" or "competitive advantage." Instead, "it was the ability to diagnose a workflow, create a solution, and build a go-to-market around all of that," he said. "AI promises to disintermediate the piece about writing the code, not the rest. But of course, the read-through from this to the stock market is case by case."

For investors looking to buy beaten-down plays, there are opportunities in subsectors, like infrastructure and cybersecurity companies, that analysts say are likely to survive disruption from AI.

Focus on infrastructure over applications

KeyBanc Capital Markets analyst Jackson Ader sees a better future for infrastructure software companies, which provide the back-end platforms that power software systems, than for application software companies, which offer the front-end software that users and businesses interact with.

"Infrastructure software is going to be harder to replicate and harder to custom build and custom code," Ader told MarketWatch.

He called out Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), SAP (SAP), Salesforce (CRM), Adobe (ADBE) and ServiceNow (NOW) as companies that could stay entrenched in corporate environments.

Melissa Otto, head of research at S&P Global Visible Alpha, noted that investors may be wondering if Microsoft "is an interesting opportunity or not" in light of how rapidly its multiple has contracted. With the stock diving 13.9% in two weeks to wipe out $481.3 billion in market capitalization, it now trades at 22.3-times forward earnings, according to FactSet data, versus its five-year average of 29.5.

Yet KeyBanc's Ader believes there are opportunities for Microsoft to have positive tailwinds from AI. He cautioned, however, that the company's valuation "isn't necessarily a catalyst," or enough of a reason for people to buy the stock, and noted that Microsoft has underperformed its megacap peers in 2025 and in 2026. "We've liked it for a little while, and we've been wrong for a while."

Rahul Chopra, an analyst at Berenberg, also looked to dispel the "AI eats software" narrative. He said data- and cloud-infrastructure software companies like Palantir Technologies (PLTR), Snowflake (SNOW), Cloudflare (NET), MongoDB (MDB), and Datadog (DDOG) have been the "clear relative winners" for software, given these companies are more directly connected to AI workloads compared with more traditional application-layer companies.

He noted that the software-as-a-service companies that have performed the weakest in the past year are those at most risk from AI, which include names like Workday (WDAY), Atlassian (TEAM), HubSpot (HUBS) and Monday.com (MNDY).

You can't 'vibe code' cybersecurity

The Amplify Cybersecurity ETF HACK bounced 3.3% on Friday but still lost 2.5% this week, which struck some analysts as unwarranted given how technical cybersecurity products can be.

"I don't know why anyone would want to try to write their own security code. ... You need someone who is more knowledgeable in the subject matter to write that software for you," said Needham's Berg. "It would be pretty much the last bucket I think a company would ever try to write on their own."

Berenberg's Chopra also believes the worries that AI will disrupt cybersecurity companies are "unfounded" despite the fact that the subsector has been pulled into the broader selloff.

"AI is deeply embedded in the upsell momentum for cybersecurity vendors" he said. So the companies "naturally benefit" from AI monetization, especially given tailwinds for the subsector from rising geopolitical and supply-chain risks.

His top picks include Zscaler (ZS), Okta (OKTA) and CrowdStrike (CRWD), as well as SailPoint (SAIL), Rubrik (RBRK), and SentinelOne (S).

The safety of HR and payroll software

Needham's Berg also notes that one pocket of software that has irrationally been swept up in the selloff this week is human-resources and payroll software. He says this pocket of the sector, which includes companies like Paylocity (PCTY) and Paycom (PAYC), is not likely to be easily replaced by AI due to its complexity and stakes.

"This thesis that companies are just going to vibe code their own software - it's indefensible until we actually see them do it."

He noted that the complexities for payroll software are too many to easily be replaced by AI.

"If some of these vendors, like Salesforce or Workday or ServiceNow, if they can infuse AI and the customers buy it, that'll change the narrative," Berg said. "I do believe we have a chance to see that in the back half of this year."

He added that it's "a bit ironic" that the software selloff has been triggered by product launches and demos from Anthropic, Google and OpenAI.

"Either AI is eating software, or returns on AI investments are insufficient - both cannot be true," Berg said.

-Hannah Pedone

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

name: IBM
title: Big Tech firms are beginning to resemble IBM during the 1960s | Bitget News
description:
According to Bloomberg Intelligence, the market for custom AI chips could reach $122 billion by 2033, driven by these ongoing efforts.
IBM’s Legacy and the Return of Vertical Integration IBM set the standard for vertical integration in the 1960s by manufacturing the components for its mainframe computers.
While Amazon still relies heavily on Nvidia GPUs for its AI cloud services, it is increasingly emphasizing the benefits of its proprietary hardware.
Challenges Ahead for Vertical Integration Despite the momentum, Jay Goldberg of Seaport cautions that the current trend toward vertical integration may be reaching its practical limits, and not every tech giant will be able to keep up.
“Designing cutting-edge chips is extremely costly,” he said, “and only a handful of companies have the resources to pursue it.”
text:
The Shift Toward Vertical Integration in the AI Era

As the competition to lead the rapidly expanding artificial intelligence sector intensifies, major technology companies are adopting strategies reminiscent of IBM’s approach in the 1960s.

Leading cloud providers such as Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) are all at different stages of designing proprietary AI chips for their data centers, aiming to enhance their cloud and software services. Alphabet, which is currently the most advanced in this area, is reportedly considering selling its Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) to Meta, positioning itself as a direct competitor to Nvidia (NVDA), the dominant force in AI hardware.

According to Bloomberg Intelligence, the market for custom AI chips could reach $122 billion by 2033, driven by these ongoing efforts.

Beyond chip development, tech giants are also investing in infrastructure. Microsoft and Amazon, for example, are putting resources into dark fiber—unused fiber-optic lines already installed underground—to support their operations, as noted by RBC Capital Markets analyst Jonathan Atkin. Google and Meta also maintain their own cable networks, though they continue to purchase additional capacity from external suppliers. These connections are vital for linking data centers and serving enterprise clients.

This renewed focus on building proprietary hardware to support core software offerings marks a return to vertical integration—a business model first popularized by industrial magnates in the 19th century and later adopted by IBM during the digital age.

IBM’s Legacy and the Return of Vertical Integration

IBM set the standard for vertical integration in the 1960s by manufacturing the components for its mainframe computers. The company believed that producing its own specialized parts would not only improve its products but also boost profitability and protect against supply chain disruptions. This strategy proved highly effective: by 1985, IBM represented over half of the computer industry’s total market value, as highlighted by Carliss Y. Baldwin in "Design Rules."

However, IBM’s dominance waned in the 1990s as semiconductor production costs dropped and new competitors like Microsoft and Intel emerged. By the turn of the millennium, IBM had moved away from its vertically integrated model.

AI’s Influence on Today’s Tech Giants

Much like the rise of computers once drove IBM’s integration strategy, the explosion of AI—especially since the debut of ChatGPT in late 2022—has prompted today’s cloud leaders to follow a similar path. The high price and limited supply of Nvidia’s chips have encouraged companies to develop their own AI hardware, which is often more cost-effective and better tailored to their software needs.

Seaport analyst Jay Goldberg notes, “Hyperscalers are acutely aware of the risks of relying on a single supplier for AI computing power. This has created a strong incentive for them to develop their own silicon.”

Meta has started testing its own AI chips for model training and recently acquired Rivos, a chip startup, to accelerate its semiconductor ambitions. Google’s TPUs have become so advanced that companies like Anthropic, OpenAI, and even Meta have entered into major cloud agreements to access them. Meanwhile, Microsoft introduced its Maia 200 chip after a lengthy development period.

During a recent tour of Amazon’s chip laboratory and data center in Austin, Texas, the company showcased its UltraServers, which feature Amazon’s latest in-house GPU (Trainium), its Graviton CPU, and custom networking hardware. While Amazon still relies heavily on Nvidia GPUs for its AI cloud services, it is increasingly emphasizing the benefits of its proprietary hardware.

Paul Roberts, director of technology at Amazon Web Services, shared that the Trainium3 chip can offer up to 60% better price-performance for cloud customers compared to traditional GPUs for inference tasks.

“We’re seeing strong evidence that using custom chips, rather than generic GPUs, enables the creation of highly efficient processors and accelerators that deliver significant energy savings,” Roberts explained.

These energy efficiencies are expected to become even more important as the rapid expansion of AI data centers begins to strain power resources.

Challenges Ahead for Vertical Integration

Despite the momentum, Jay Goldberg of Seaport cautions that the current trend toward vertical integration may be reaching its practical limits, and not every tech giant will be able to keep up. “Designing cutting-edge chips is extremely costly,” he said, “and only a handful of companies have the resources to pursue it.”

text:
SHANIKO, Ore. (KTVZ) — Ralph Lauren has unveiled the official Opening and Closing Ceremony uniforms for the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Teams for the Winter Games Milano Cortina 2026. The uniforms feature wool sourced from the Shaniko Wool Company, based in Maupin, Ore.

Lauren has served as the Official Outfitter for Team USA since 2008. All apparel worn by U.S. athletes during the official Olympic and Paralympic ceremonies is manufactured in the United States through partnerships with local manufacturers and materials providers.

The 2026 uniforms feature several pieces made from wool provided by Shaniko Wool Company. This includes the toggle coat, pants, sweater, mittens and earflap hat for the Opening Ceremony. The Oregon-based company also provided wool for the sweaters and beanies used in the Closing Ceremony.

Shaniko Wool Company was founded in 2018 by Dan and Jeanne Carver. The company is based in Maupin, Ore., and operates as a collection of family ranches across the American West. It currently serves as a primary U.S. source of American Merino wool certified under the Responsible Wool Standard.

The company's roots trace back to the Imperial Stock Ranch, which was established in north central Oregon in 1871. In 1999, the Carvers developed a new business model by selling wool directly to textile brands that prioritized land stewardship and transparency. The Imperial Stock Ranch became the first ranch in the world to receive the Responsible Wool Standard certification in 2017.

Founders Dan and Carver have emphasized a focus on environmental outcomes through their work. "Our mission remains simple: to see the land win, while delivering responsibly grown wool to the world," the company stated regarding its objectives.

The wool used in the uniforms is 100% transparent and carries a double NATIVA Regen and Responsible Wool Standard certification. The company uses regenerative farming practices intended to maintain healthy ecosystems and soil health.

The founders described their long-term approach to land management as essential for the future of the industry. "Sustainability is more than a trend for us—it's been a way of life for decades and necessary to ensure our future on the land as stewards of its soil, water, grasslands and the grazing animals vital to its health," the company stated.

The history of the ranch and the Carvers' work in the textile industry is documented at the Columbia Gorge Discovery Center & Museum. In 2025, the museum opened a permanent exhibit titled "A Sense of Place" that covers the formation of Shaniko Wool Company and the legacy of the Imperial Stock Ranch.

The Olympic and Paralympic Winter Games Milano Cortina 2026 are scheduled for 2026. Team USA athletes will wear the Lauren uniforms during the official Opening and Closing ceremonies.

text:
HP and Redington India have inaugurated a Center of Excellence (CoE) in Chennai to accelerate the adoption of digital printing technologies and support the evolving needs of India’s print industry.

The 20,000 square feet facility will function as a hub for live technology demonstrations, professional training, process optimization and consulting services, allowing customers to explore the full capabilities of HP Indigo digital printing solutions.

The center will also conduct programmes aimed at helping brands and print buyers better utilise digital printing to stay competitive in a rapidly changing market.

The facility was inaugurated by Arnon Goldman, general manager, Industrial Print GTM, Asia Pacific and Japan, HP Inc, Pawan Chauhan, country business manager, HP Industrial and Inkjet Business Solutions, HP India, along with VS Hariharan, Group CEO, Redington and Ramesh KS, vice president – Digital Printing Group, Redington, in the presence of customers, industry leaders, associations and solution partners.

Pawan Chauhan said, “The launch of the Centre of Excellence reflects our long-term commitment to the Indian digital printing ecosystem and our focus on building skills for the future of work and driving innovation. It is also a moment to celebrate 21 years of trust and collaboration between HP and Redington. Together with Redington, we are creating a platform that empowers Indian print buyers and businesses to adopt new technologies, explore innovative applications, and grow their businesses.”

Arnon Goldman said, “India is at a remarkable moment in its digital journey, with a rapidly growing economy and increasing demand for high-quality, personalized print. This has presented an opportune moment to inaugurate the Centre of Excellence, providing our customers with end-to-end solutions under one dedicated roof and supporting them in strengthening their capabilities in this dynamic market.”

Highlighting the broader manufacturing opportunity, VS Hariharan said, “The future of manufacturing will be built upon flexible, digital-first platforms. As customer expectations evolve, this COE initiative positions Redington to deepen engagement with customers, expand solution-led revenues, and accelerate the adoption of advance digital printing technologies, including HP Indigo and HP Industrial 3D Printing solutions. The Center of Excellence unlocks access to world-class HP Indigo and HP Industrial 3D printing platforms, while strengthening OEM-led innovation and ecosystem partnerships—reinforcing our role as a growth enabler across the manufacturing and printing industry.”

Ramesh KS added, “The Center of Excellence is designed as a hands-on, experience-led platform where customers can clearly experience the real-world impact of advanced digital printing and industrial 3D printing solutions. The facility brings together industry-leading solutions, complemented by AI-enabled workflows, creative design capabilities, and new application development”.

The technology showcase at the centre includes live demonstrations of the HP Indigo 18K B2-format digital press and the HP Indigo 7K A3-format press. The Indigo 7K will be fully operational for demonstrations and training, enabling print service providers to experience digital production workflows suited for short runs, customised jobs and high-value applications. Meanwhile, the Indigo 18K demonstrates how commercial printers can scale digital production with faster makereadies and manage diverse applications more efficiently.

The centre also houses finishing equipment along with prepress, post-press and web-to-print solutions serving commercial print, photo, publishing and labels and packaging segments, highlighting the versatility and quality achievable through HP Indigo digital printing technology.

name: Eli Lilly
title: Eli Lilly's 25% Growth Outlook: Assessing Scalability Against a Recalibrated $1 Trillion Market
description:
This kind of volume growth is the bedrock of the scalability thesis.
The company's 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $33.50 to $35.00 implies massive earnings leverage from the projected revenue ramp.
The Pricing Wild Card: Margin Pressure vs. Volume Growth The most critical risk to Lilly's 25% growth thesis is a familiar one in a hyper-competitive market: pricing erosion.
The consensus is a Moderate Buy with an average price target of $1,162.75, but there is clear divergence.
For the growth investor, the critical metric is whether volume expansion can consistently outpace the 25% sales growth target.
text:
The investment case for Eli LillyLLY-- hinges on a simple, audacious question: can a company that just hit a $1 trillion market cap keep scaling at a blistering pace? The answer, for now, appears to be a resounding yes. The company's 2026 sales projection of $80 billion to $83 billion represents a 25% increase from its 2025 revenue of $65.2 billion. That's a growth rate that would make most tech giants envious, and it's being guided by a business model that has already demonstrated extraordinary execution.

The proof is in the quarterly numbers. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Lilly's revenue surged 43% to $19.3 billion, driven by a 46% increase in volume for its key products. This wasn't a one-off beat; it was a continuation of a powerful trend. Combined sales of its tirzepatide drugs, Mounjaro and Zepbound, hit $36.5 billion for the full year, making them the world's best-selling drug. This kind of volume growth is the bedrock of the scalability thesis. It shows LillyLLY-- isn't just selling more of the same-it's rapidly expanding the patient base for its metabolic disease franchise.

The long-term market potential provides the runway for this ambition. While the obesity drug market is still evolving, the global forecast has been recalibrated upward, with one projection now placing the long-term market at $139 billion. Lilly's 2026 target of $80-83 billion would still represent less than 60% of that total addressable market, leaving significant room for growth even if the company captures a dominant share. The company's aggressive expansion of manufacturing capacity and its new U.S. government agreement to expand access to obesity medicines are concrete steps to meet that demand.

Of course, the path isn't without friction. CFO Lucas Montarce acknowledged that price is expected to be a drag on growth in the low- to mid-teens this year, citing factors like government pricing deals and Medicaid concessions. But the company's confidence in volume growth to offset this is clear. The 2026 guidance, which easily outpaced Street expectations, stands in stark contrast to the market's recent jitters. It signals a management team that sees a massive, growing TAM and believes its operational scale can capture it. For the growth investor, that's the core of the thesis: a $1 trillion company with a 25% growth target, backed by a product portfolio that is scaling at a pace few could have imagined a year ago.

Manufacturing and Distribution: The Scalability Engine

The real test of Lilly's 25% growth target is not in its financial guidance, but in its ability to manufacture and distribute the volume required to hit it. The company's 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $33.50 to $35.00 implies massive earnings leverage from the projected revenue ramp. To achieve that, Lilly's operational infrastructure must scale in lockstep with its product pipeline.

The foundation is already being built. Management highlighted expanded manufacturing capacity as a key enabler, a critical investment to meet the soaring demand for tirzepatide drugs. This focus on scaling production is the literal engine of the scalability thesis. Without it, even the most optimistic sales forecasts would stall at the factory gate. The company's confidence in volume growth to offset pricing pressures-seen in the 46% volume surge last quarter-depends entirely on this physical capacity.

Pipeline catalysts will provide the next wave of demand. Regulatory submissions for orforglipron, an oral GLP-1, and retatrutide are poised to expand the company's metabolic disease portfolio. These are not just incremental products; they represent potential new revenue streams and ways to capture patients who may not be candidates for injectables. Positive Phase 3 data for these drugs, as well as for combination therapies, strengthens the long-term growth narrative and justifies continued investment in distribution networks.

A major strategic move to drive volume is the agreement with the U.S. government to expand access to obesity medicines. This deal is a potential game-changer for the cash-pay consumer market. By opening access to millions of Americans, it directly addresses a key barrier to adoption and creates a massive, new volume driver. For a growth investor, this is a powerful signal that Lilly is not just chasing the existing market but actively expanding the total addressable market through policy and partnership.

The bottom line is that Lilly's financial targets are only as credible as its operational execution. The company is betting heavily on its manufacturing and distribution systems to deliver the volume needed for its EPS targets. With expanded capacity, a robust pipeline, and a landmark government agreement, the infrastructure is being built to support its ambitious growth. The coming quarters will show whether this scalability engine can keep pace with the company's soaring ambitions.

The Pricing Wild Card: Margin Pressure vs. Volume Growth

The most critical risk to Lilly's 25% growth thesis is a familiar one in a hyper-competitive market: pricing erosion. CEO David Ricks called lower realized prices a "bit of a wild card" for the short- and mid-term outlook, a sentiment echoed by the market's reaction to rival Novo Nordisk's warning of potential revenue declines. The evidence is clear. In the fourth quarter, a 5% decrease due to lower realized prices partially offset a staggering 46% volume surge. This dynamic is now a formal part of the company's guidance, with CFO Lucas Montarce stating "price is expected to be a drag on growth in the low- to mid-teens" for 2026.

The sources of this pressure are multifaceted. They include the landmark U.S. government agreement to sell Zepbound and orforglipron at cut-rate prices, direct-to-patient pricing plans, and lower Medicaid pricing for later-lifecycle products. These are strategic moves to expand access and patient volume, but they come at a near-term cost to the top line. The company's Q4 2025 gross margin as a percent of revenue held steady at 83.2%, a figure that management noted was maintained despite the pricing headwinds, thanks to favorable product mix and cost improvements. This stability is a positive sign for profitability, but it also underscores the pressure being applied.

The key question for scalability is whether volume growth can fully offset this pricing drag. Management's confidence is explicit: "We believe these price concessions will be more than offset by increased volume over time." This is the core of the investment bet. The recent market volatility illustrates the tension. When Novo's pricing fears hit, Lilly's shares fell 5%, but the company's own strong earnings and guidance prompted a 10% surge the next day. This rebound shows the market is weighing the volume story against the pricing risk.

Some analysts argue the impact may be less severe than feared. They point out that price cuts can boost volumes, and that direct-to-consumer access strategies may help stabilize the cash-pay market. The recalibration of the obesity drug market forecast-from a peak of $150 billion to estimates now around $80 billion-reflects this uncertainty. Yet even with a lower TAM, Lilly's 2026 sales target of $80-83 billion would still represent a dominant share. The scalability engine depends on manufacturing and distribution keeping pace with that volume expansion, regardless of the per-unit price.

The bottom line is that pricing is a known, material headwind. For the growth investor, the critical takeaway is that Lilly is not just scaling volume-it is actively engineering it through policy deals and access programs. The company's ability to maintain its gross margin while navigating this pressure, and to accelerate volume growth to fully absorb the price concessions, will determine whether the 25% growth target is a realistic milestone or a stretch. The next few quarters will provide the first real test of this delicate balance.

Valuation, Analyst Sentiment, and Key Catalysts

The investment case for Eli Lilly now sits at a critical juncture. The company's audacious 25% growth target has been met with a mix of cautious optimism and significant skepticism, reflected in a wide range of analyst price targets. The consensus is a Moderate Buy with an average price target of $1,162.75, but there is clear divergence. Firms like Jefferies have set a bullish $1,300 target, while others, such as Morgan Stanley, have raised their view to $1,290. This spread indicates a market divided on the ultimate ceiling for the stock, with the primary debate centering on the sustainability of volume growth versus the reality of pricing pressure.

The key watchpoint for validating the scalability thesis is the interplay between these two forces. Management has explicitly stated that price is expected to be a drag on growth in the low- to mid-teens for 2026. The company's confidence rests on its ability to accelerate volume growth to fully offset this headwind. For the growth investor, the critical metric is whether volume expansion can consistently outpace the 25% sales growth target. Sustained volume growth above that threshold would be the clearest signal that Lilly's manufacturing and distribution engine is scaling effectively and that its market penetration is accelerating as planned.

The next major catalyst is the company's own performance against its 2026 guidance. The midpoint of the sales range-$81.5 billion-represents a 25% increase from 2025. Given that Lilly already grew sales by 45% last year, hitting this target would require a significant deceleration. Any deviation from this guidance, particularly a downward revision, would likely trigger a sharp reassessment of the growth narrative. The market's recent volatility underscores this sensitivity. When rival Novo Nordisk warned of potential revenue declines, Lilly's shares fell 5%. The subsequent 10% surge on strong earnings and guidance shows the stock's momentum is tied directly to the volume story.

In summary, the path forward is defined by a few concrete events. Investors must monitor quarterly volume growth to see if it can absorb pricing concessions. They must also watch for any changes to the 2026 sales guidance, as the company's ability to meet or exceed its midpoint is critical for maintaining investor confidence. The wide analyst price targets highlight the uncertainty, but they also point to a clear upside if Lilly can demonstrate that its scalability engine is still accelerating.

name: Delta Airlines
title: Wheels Up sets Q4 earnings release date
description:
Delta Air Lines-backed Wheels Up will release its fourth quarter and full year 2025 financial results on Feb. 19, 2026.
Wheels Up Financials If it’s good to have a big, financially strong backer for Wheels Up, there is still work to do.
As of Q3 2025, Delta held a 37% stake in Wheels Up.
Under Delta, Wheels Up has moved from a purely programmatic offering to an integrated solutions approach.
In December, Wheels Up launched online booking of Delta flights for members who can use funds for those flights.
text:
Delta Air Lines-backed Wheels Up will release its fourth quarter and full year 2025 financial results on Feb. 19, 2026.

The company announced the date yesterday.

CEO George Mattson, a longtime Delta board member before becoming CEO of the private jet company in October 2023, said in announcing Q3 2025 numbers, “We expect our fourth quarter financial results to be the best since starting our transformation two years ago, setting the stage for accelerating improvement as we close the year and head into 2026.”

While Wheels Up hopes to show progress in reducing losses, Delta has already released its earnings.

Delta Air Lines CEO Ed Bastian said of its earnings, “Our industry-leading performance delivered for our customers and our employees, while creating value for our owners, consistent with our long-term financial framework.”

He added, “We generated $5 billion of pre-tax profit with a double-digit operating margin and record free cash flow of $4.6 billion, all while navigating a challenging environment.”

What’s more, Bastian noted, “2026 is off to a strong start with top-line growth accelerating on consumer and corporate demand.”

He predicted, “For the full year, we expect to deliver margin expansion and earnings growth of 20 percent year-over-year.”

Delta’s financial results are important to Wheels Up, as Bastian has said the private jet company is a key strategic element of its approach to the premium travel market.

In a 2024 interview, Bastian said, “The overarching opportunity for us was the strength of (Delta’s) affluent customers, our corporate businesses, and our premium customer base. It’s driving all of the growth of Delta premium as a whole.”

Delta had operating revenue of $63.4 billion in 2025, with operating income of $5.8 billion, and pre-tax income of $6.2 billion.

Of particular note, Delta reported that corporate sales were up in the high single digits year over year in the December quarter, similar to the September quarter.

Delta added that recent corporate survey results indicate that nearly 90 percent of companies expect their travel volume to increase or remain steady in 2026.

Bastian has identified Wheels Up as being a differentiator in Delta’s offering to corporate accounts.

Wheels Up Financials

If it’s good to have a big, financially strong backer for Wheels Up, there is still work to do.

Its Q3 earnings showed revenue decreased to $185.5 million from $193.9 million year over year.

Loss from operations increased to $61.3 million from $41.8 million.

Net loss increased from $57.7 million to $83.7 million.

Adjusted EBITDA loss increased to $23.2 million from $19.9 million.

Adjusted EBITDAR loss increased to $19.6 million from $11.6 million.

Wheels Up is seeking to cut $70 million in costs.

It also received a warning from the NYSE.

The notice came after its stock price fell below $1 for 30 consecutive trading days in December.

It closed at 67 cents today, giving it a market cap of $485 million.

As of Q3 2025, Delta held a 37% stake in Wheels Up.

Wheels Up Transition

At the time, Mattson said its financials were being weighed down by expenses related to its fleet modernization program.

Fleet modernization is key to making Wheels Up more attractive to Delta’s corporate customers.

Mattson says the fleet of refurbished Phenom 300s and Challenger 300s makes Wheels Up’s product attractive to that market.

He added that the GAAP financials don’t accurately reflect Wheels Up’s transition.

Under Delta, Wheels Up has moved from a purely programmatic offering to an integrated solutions approach.

In a letter to investors, Mattson wrote, “Total Gross Bookings, a measure of gross spend by members and customers across our broader product portfolio, grew by 5% year over year, driven by 14% growth in on-demand charter offerings.”

Gross bookings account for the value of off-fleet on-demand charter bookings through its Air Partner brokerage, rather than just the margin between what the flight is sold for and what Wheels Up pays to the operator.

Those made-to-measure solutions are a key part of the integration with Delta.

Air Partner has been selling charter flights in Europe to flyers arriving from the U.S. in Delta One business class.

Last month, the company integrated the Wheels Up and Air Partner sales teams.

In terms of the numbers, Mattson noted, “We improved our Gross Profit profile and expanded Adjusted Contribution Margins from essentially breakeven at the start of our transformation just over two years ago to mid-to-high teens margins over the last year. As a result, our Adjusted EBITDAR losses have reduced by approximately 25% year-to-date.”

Wheels Up, Delta Integration

Mattson also said that Wheels Up is beginning to see the benefits of its integrated sales approach with Delta.

Corporate Membership jet card sales hit a quarterly record of $62 million in Q3 2025, up more than 15% year-over-year.

It also represented 49% of all membership fund sales in the quarter, a record.

In December, Wheels Up launched online booking of Delta flights for members who can use funds for those flights.

Wheels Up has also been working to improve reliability.

In January, it recorded 14 consecutive days without a cancellation.

Last July, Bastian said there had been “meaningful progress” at the private jet company.

title: Why OpenAI's Early Super Bowl Strategy Should Get Small Business Owners' Attention
description:
Ahead of its national Super Bowl spot, OpenAI has released three regional ads that preview the campaign’s direction.
Each profile centers on an American small business—a seed farm, a metal salvage yard, and a family-run tamale shop—using ChatGPT as part of daily operations rather than as a novelty.
For small business owners, these early rollouts do more than set tone.
Showing real work creates relevance Many owners know AI exists.
What most people don’t know ChatGPT can do The three regional spots also surface a critical insight for adoption: most people underestimate what ChatGPT supports.
text:
Ahead of its national Super Bowl spot, OpenAI has released three regional ads that preview the campaign’s direction. Each profile centers on an American small business—a seed farm, a metal salvage yard, and a family-run tamale shop—using ChatGPT as part of daily operations rather than as a novelty.

For small business owners, these early rollouts do more than set tone. They clarify where AI actually earns its keep.

Showing real work creates relevance

Many owners know AI exists. Many still struggle to see how it supports actual work. OpenAI addresses that gap by placing the technology inside recognizable business moments.

The regional spots follow founders navigating planning cycles, operational complexity, growth decisions, and time scarcity. ChatGPT appears during moments that require judgment: organizing information, thinking through scenarios, evaluating trade-offs, and preparing for what comes next.

The ads avoid explanation and instruction, relying instead on demonstration. Viewers learn by watching people use the tool in context, which makes the value more straightforward and easier to imagine inside their own businesses.

What most people don’t know ChatGPT can do

The three regional spots also surface a critical insight for adoption: most people underestimate what ChatGPT supports.

name: Eli Lilly
title: ANTITRUST—7th Cir.: Pilgrim's Pride, Sysco did not have binding $50 million global settlement
description:
The Seventh Circuit reasoned the settlement the district court had confirmed on a Pilgrim’s motion for summary judgment suffered from four unresolved material terms.
In September 2016, purchasers of broiler chickens launched a class action against certain producers of broilers (Broilers litigation).
Among other circumstances, in October 2020 Pilgrim’s Pride Corp. (Pilgrim’s) entered into a plea agreement with the Department of Justice Antitrust Division with respect to the criminal charges related to the price fixing.
A year after the September 9, 2022 “We accept” email, Pilgrim’s moved to enforce that putative Broilers settlement agreement as binding as of that email.
Christopher George Michel (Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan, LLP) for Pilgrim’s Pride Corp. Companies: Carina Ventures LLC; Pilgrim’s Pride Corp. MainStory: TopStory Antitrust IllinoisNews IndianaNews WisconsinNews
text:
By Justin Marcus Smith, J.D.

A concurring opinion suggested the procedurally complex situation presented a cautionary tale about litigation funding.

In a facet of the broiler chickens (Broilers) antitrust litigation, as further complicated by a litigation funding agreement, the United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit held Pilgrim’s Pride Corp. (Pilgrim’s) did not have a binding $50 million global settlement with meat purchaser Sysco Corp. The Seventh Circuit reasoned the settlement the district court had confirmed on a Pilgrim’s motion for summary judgment suffered from four unresolved material terms. Pilgrim’s itself had also repeatedly said it would not enter into a settlement that did not qualify under a “Judgment Sharing Agreement”, and that alone defeated Pilgrim’s argument. The Seventh Circuit reversed a ruling upholding the judgment sharing agreement by the federal district court in Illinois and remanded (Carina Ventures LLC v. Pilgrim’s Pride Corp. https://business.cch.com/ipld/InreGoogleGenAICopyrightLitig20250911.pdf, No. 25-1110 (7th Cir. Feb. 5, 2026)).

Background. In September 2016, purchasers of broiler chickens launched a class action against certain producers of broilers (Broilers litigation). The purchasers sued a collection of industrial chicken meat producers, plus Eli Lilly & Co, which produces subscription reports about the industry, alleging a conspiracy to fix prices between 2008 and 2016 in violation of §1 of the Sherman Act. See In re Broiler Chicken Antitrust Litig., 290 F. Supp. 3D 772, 779–87 (N.D. Ill. 2017) (summarizing history of Broilers litigation).

Among other circumstances, in October 2020 Pilgrim’s Pride Corp. (Pilgrim’s) entered into a plea agreement with the Department of Justice Antitrust Division with respect to the criminal charges related to the price fixing. There were also two parallel class actions in the beef and pork industries in the District of Minnesota.

There were various settlement agreements in the Broilers class action.At an early point, Pilgrim’s and many other defendants entered into a “Judgment Sharing Agreement” (JSA) to cope with the joint and several liability that can apply to all conspirators under antitrust law. The agreement allowed a Broilers defendant to settle with a plaintiff so as to escape possible joint and several liability for that plaintiff’s claims against other defendants that succeed in the future. To get that benefit, a settlement must be “qualified” under the JSA.

To be qualified under the JSA, a settling plaintiff must (1) agree to subtract damages stemming from its purchases with the settling defendant in any later settlements with or judgments against other defendants; and (2) provide written notice of its settlement to other JSA defendants within seven days of execution. Pilgrim’s was a party to the JSA.

Settlement talks. In about August 2022, attorneys for Pilgrim’s and Sysco Corp. (Sysco), the original plaintiff in the instant matter, ended a call having reached an agreement, at least in principle, for Sysco to release its Broilers, Beef, and Pork claims for $50 million.

However, complicating matters, Sysco had obtained litigation funding from Burford Capital (Capital). One condition of the Burford Capital funding was that Sysco “shall not” accept a settlement offer without Burford’s prior written consent.

The next day, a Pilgrim’s attorney confirmed the $50 million global settlement. Some days later, on September 9, 2022, a Sysco attorney later responded, “We accept,” and requested that both parties’ counsel work out the release. Other terms were not agreed at the time of this critical exchange, but the parties disagreed about the details of what was and was not agreed. Later communications showed negotiations continued about assignments, allocations, a “most favored nation” (MFN) clause, and JSA compliance. Assignments were amounts of Sysco claims assigned to other parties. Allocations were percentages of the total settlement sum for each of the three Broiler, Beef, and Pork cases.

Pilgrim’s took a stab at drafting a settlement agreement. First drafts sent to Sysco noted the allocations were not final. All drafts contained an integration clause in which both parties agreed to refrain from bringing any claim based on an alleged agreement not fully executed.

At length, a Pilgrim’s outside counsel threatened to walk away over Sysco proposed edits narrowing the JSA. Pilgrim’s also insisted that a key settlement ratio pertinent to the MFN clause was not final without confirmation of Sysco’s assignment data. The parties continued to negotiate.

In December 2022, Burford, having already filed an expedited request for arbitration in September, asserted its right to withhold its consent for Sysco to settle. Burford formally objected to the $50 million global terms. Burford moved for a temporary restraining order (TRO) and preliminary injunction from the arbitral tribunal to stop Sysco from executing the agreement, and the tribunal issued an arbitral TRO.

Sysco counsel told Pilgrim’s counsel that Burford had won a TRO such that Sysco could not finalize the settlement, but Sysco and Pilgrim’s continued to make small edits to the draft agreements, including language about the ongoing Burford arbitration.

On December 22, 2022, Pilgrim’s sent final versions of the agreements for Sysco to execute and followed-up that it would not keep its offer open indefinitely. A putative Pilgrim’s threat to pursue litigation if Buford did not lift the TRO was ambiguous about whether it pertained to litigating the antitrust claims or to asking the court to enforce a settlement that arguably already existed.

In March 2023, after a multi-day evidentiary hearing, the arbitral tribunal issued a preliminary injunction preventing Sysco from executing the proposed settlement agreements. Pilgrim’s outside counsel reiterated a commitment to settle on the terms negotiated up to that point because of the ongoing business relationship between Pilgrim’s and Sysco, but that counselor did not assert the parties had a binding agreement, and there was no execution.

In June 2023, Burford and Sysco resolved their disputes. As part of that settlement, Sysco assigned its claims in the Broilers, Beef, and Pork cases to Burford’s affiliate, Carina Ventures, LLC, which became the plaintiff-appellant in this appeal.

Enforcement motion. A year after the September 9, 2022 “We accept” email, Pilgrim’s moved to enforce that putative Broilers settlement agreement as binding as of that email. The district court granted Pilgrim’s motion to enforce, writing that the “heart” of the agreement, and its only two material terms, were the release of all claims in exchange for the $50 million global sum both parties agreed.

The district court also relied on the finalized draft agreements which it said memorialized additional, immaterial terms, even though drafts were never executed. The district court rejected Carina’s laches argument that Pilgrim’s waited too long to enforce the agreement, as well as the jurisdictional argument that the court could not enforce a global settlement agreement that involved the Beef and Pork claims pending in the District of Minnesota.

Carina appeal. Carina appealed arguing the district court decision operated as an injunction, but the Seventh Circuit dismissed that appeal because on the basis it did not have jurisdiction because the decision did not operate as an appealable junction. In re Broiler Chicken Antitrust Litig., Nos. 24-2100 & 24-2202, 2024 WL 5153588, at *1 (7th Cir. Aug. 14, 2024).

Checks deposited. In July 2024, Pilgrim’s sent three separate checks to Sysco totaling $50 million, one for each of Sysco’s Broilers, Beef, and Pork settlements. The amounts corresponded to the allocations in the unexecuted settlement agreements. Sysco deposited the checks and transferred the funds to Carina, but Carina maintained (1) the payments were not valid under the settlement draft agreement; (2) did not affect Carina’s right to pursue appeal; and (3) it stood ready to return the money to Pilgrim’s.

Pilgrim’s moved for summary judgment on Carina’s broiler claims, without framing the motion that way, such that it amounted to a motion for summary judgment on a new affirmative defense of accord and satisfaction.

Appealed judgment. In the Broilers judgment, on the instant Carina appeal of the claims Sysco assigned to it, the district court granted Pilgrim’s motion for summary judgment and entered a separate judgment under Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(b). The district court relied on its earlier decision that a binding settlement agreement existed and found Pilgrim’s had fulfilled its obligations under the settlement by paying the $50 million due. Pilgrim’s then successfully moved for summary judgment on Carina’s Beef and Pork claims in the District of Minnesota.

De novo review. The Seventh Circuit reviewed both the summary judgment order and the underlying decision to enforce de novo, where the district court had found, without holding an evidentiary hearing, that the record facts showed an unambiguous meeting of the minds in settlement.

The Seventh Circuit said the central legal question was a contextual one: how to decide which terms the parties thought material.

Inchoate settlement. The Seventh Circuit found the parties negotiations and actions refuted the Pilgrim’s theory that the settlement was a simple exchange of $50 million for the release of all Sysco antitrust claims. Terms the parties negotiated for months after September 9, 2022, directly affected the value of the deal and the scope of the release. The terms were both material and unresolved at the time of the September 9, 2022 “We accept” email. The terms were compliance with the JSA, scope of Sysco assignments, the MFN clause, and the case allocations.

Under Illinois law, even if both parties meant to be bound, there is no enforceable contract if, as here, material terms are left unresolved. A bare-bones email exchange like the one here might suffice for getting at the “heart” of an agreement in a simpler context, but Pilgrim’s itself had repeatedly said it would not enter into a settlement that did not qualify under the JSA. The JSA “qualified settlement” language was so important that Pilgrim’s threatened to walk away after the parties supposedly reached a binding agreement. That alone defeated Pilgrim’s argument that it had a binding settlement.

More broadly, the settlement had unresolved moving parts. It was also so large in value it did not make sense that a party would agree based on an email about only two material terms, where there were actually four unresolved material terms.

Concurrence. Judge Maldonado concurred but said the opinion skimmed over much of Carina’s role in the case as well as the “complex” procedural posture of the order under review. Aside from the “procedural oddities” he said worked to Pilgrim’s disadvantage, he offered a “postmortem” explanation of what happened and called the case a “cautionary tale to any party who seeks to fund its litigation through a third party.”

The Case is No. 25-1110.

Judge: Hamilton, D.

Attorneys: Derek T. Ho (Kellogg, Hansen, Todd, Figel & Frederick PLLC) for Carina Ventures LLC. Christopher George Michel (Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan, LLP) for Pilgrim’s Pride Corp.

Companies: Carina Ventures LLC; Pilgrim’s Pride Corp.

name: Instacart
title: Myntra appoints ex-Google and Instacart executive Pramod Adiddam as CTO
description:
Fashion ecommerce major Myntra has appointed Pramod Adiddam as its chief technology officer (CTO).
Adiddam will report to CEO Nandita Sinha and oversee Myntra’s overall technology strategy, with a focus on innovation, platform resilience and long-term technology capability.
The appointment follows the exit of Myntra’s former chief technology and product officer Raghu Krishnananda, who stepped down in April last year after a five-year stint.
Net profit rose nearly 18 times to Rs 548 crore, compared with Rs 31 crore in the previous fiscal year.
Revenue from operations increased 18% year-on-year to Rs 6,043 crore from Rs 5,122 crore in FY24.
text:
Fashion ecommerce major Myntra has appointed Pramod Adiddam as its chief technology officer (CTO).

Adiddam will report to CEO Nandita Sinha and oversee Myntra’s overall technology strategy, with a focus on innovation, platform resilience and long-term technology capability.

Adiddam brings more than two decades of experience in building and operating large consumer-facing technology platforms. Before joining Myntra, he served as vice president and head of engineering at US-based grocery delivery platform Instacart, where he spent five years leading global engineering teams across platform development, marketplace expansion and product innovation.

Prior to that, he spent over 11 years at Google, most recently as head of engineering. His background includes architecting large-scale commerce systems, scaling multi-billion-dollar marketplace engines and working with advanced agentic models.

“As Myntra continues to grow in scale and depth of offerings, Pramod’s expertise in building and operating large, global platforms and his ability to combine deep technical understanding with disciplined execution will be key as we continue to strengthen our technology foundations at scale,” Sinha said.

The appointment follows the exit of Myntra’s former chief technology and product officer Raghu Krishnananda, who stepped down in April last year after a five-year stint. It is also the second senior leadership hire by Myntra this year, after the appointment of Ritesh Mishra as senior vice president and head of category and revenue last month.

On the financial front, the Bengaluru-based company reported a sharp improvement in profitability in FY25. Net profit rose nearly 18 times to Rs 548 crore, compared with Rs 31 crore in the previous fiscal year. Revenue from operations increased 18% year-on-year to Rs 6,043 crore from Rs 5,122 crore in FY24.

description:
Lois Juanita JORGENSEN OBITUARY JORGENSEN, Lois Juanita Born December 25, 1938 in Sasakwa, OK to James and Jesse Brumley passed away December 24, 1916, surrounded by family.
She was a graduate of Santa Rosa High School and worked for HP/Agilent Technologies for more than 25 years.
She is survived by her brother Coy Brumley (Connie), sister-in-law Carol Brumley and children; Barbara Armbrust (Eric), Rusty Armbrust (Lisa) and Dan Armbrust (Judy), grandchildren; Sandra Armbrust, Alicia Coombes (Jake), Richie Armbrust, Ben Budnik (Amy) and Leann Armbrust (Nick) and numerous great-grandchildren, nieces and nephews.
She was preceded in death by parents James and Jesse Brumley and seven siblings, husbands Bob Whitworth, John Jorgensen and the love of her life Dick Armbrust.
Memorial services will be held on Jan. 28, 2017, at 1:00 p.m. in the chapel at Santa Rosa Memorial Park, with a celebration of her life to follow at Finley Park, Senior Center Room 4.
